planting.note: Divide and plant pups (offsets) in spring after all frost danger has passed, in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade. In zones 5–8, mulch the rhizome heavily with 12–18 inches of straw or leaves in fall to protect from deep frost; the pseudostem dies to the ground but the rhizome re-sprouts vigorously each spring.

directions: Japanese Fiber Banana is the hardiest banana in the world, native to the Ryukyu Islands of southern Japan and widely grown worldwide for its ability to provide a lush, tropical effect even in cold climates where true tropical bananas cannot survive — the rhizome is reliably hardy to the ground in zone 5 with heavy mulching, producing new pseudostems up to 12–15 feet tall each growing season in warm, moist conditions. In zones 9–10 the pseudostem survives winter and can eventually produce small flower clusters, though the fruit is not culinary; the plant's value is entirely ornamental, providing dramatic tropical foliage and a bold vertical accent that transforms temperate gardens into apparent tropics for the summer season. Divide pups from the parent clump in spring and plant in the richest, most moisture-retentive soil available in full sun to partial shade; water generously throughout the growing season and fertilize monthly with a balanced fertilizer for maximum growth. In zones 5–7, cut the pseudostem to within 12 inches of the ground after frost and mulch the root zone with a foot or more of insulating material through winter.

planting.note: Divide and plant pups in spring after all frost danger has passed, in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to light shade. Pink Banana is one of the smaller ornamental bananas, reaching only 4–6 feet, and is well-suited to containers or small garden spaces where larger bananas would be overwhelming.

directions: Pink Banana is one of the most ornamental of the small bananas, native to the forests of Assam, India, and grown worldwide for the striking combination of its vivid pink, velvety flower bracts and small, bright pink, hairy fruits that split open when ripe to reveal sweet white flesh studded with seeds — an unusual display that makes the plant a conversation piece in any garden. Growing only 4–6 feet tall, it is well-suited to container culture, small garden spaces, and use as an accent among larger tropical foliage plants, and it blooms and fruits within its first year from a good-sized pup. In zones 8–9 the pseudostem may die to the ground in freezing weather, but the rhizome re-sprouts reliably in spring; in zones 9b–11 it is effectively evergreen. Divide pups from the parent clump in spring, plant in rich, well-drained soil in full sun, and water and fertilize generously throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in full sun. Mexican Fan Palm tolerates drought, alkaline soil, and poor sandy soil remarkably well once established; avoid wet, poorly drained sites.

directions: Mexican Fan Palm is one of the most widely planted palms in the world, native to streams and oases in Sonora and Baja California, Mexico, and now the defining skyline palm of Los Angeles, the Gulf Coast, and tropical resort landscapes worldwide — its extremely slender, tall trunk topped by a compact crown of fan-shaped fronds is the prototypical palm silhouette recognized globally. Fast-growing (1–3 feet per year once established), it reaches 50–70 feet in cultivation and is among the most drought- and heat-tolerant of all palms, thriving in poor, sandy, alkaline soil with minimal irrigation once established. Clusters of small white flowers on arching inflorescences are followed by small black fruits that attract birds; the old fronds form a brown 'skirt' of dead leaves along the trunk unless trimmed for neatness. Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil; it is intolerant of wet, clay soils and prolonged cold below 22°F but otherwise requires minimal care.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, sandy or rocky, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade. Florida Thatch Palm is slow-growing and does not tolerate frost — it is appropriate only for zones 10–11. Source only from nurseries to protect wild populations in the Florida Keys.

directions: Florida Thatch Palm is an elegant, slender native palm endemic to the coastal hammocks and rocky shorelines of the Florida Keys and extreme southern Florida, with a natural range extending through the Caribbean and Central America, where it grows in limestone soils near the coast and in coastal hammock understories. Its graceful crown of silvery-green fan-shaped fronds provides an authentically tropical appearance in zone 10–11 landscapes, and small white flowers in spring produce clusters of round white berries that are important food for Florida Keys birds, particularly white-crowned pigeons. Slow-growing, drought- and salt-tolerant once established, and tolerant of brief cold snaps to about 30°F, Florida Thatch Palm is an excellent specimen palm for small gardens or use as an understory palm beneath taller coconut or royal palms in south Florida landscapes. Wild populations are protected in Florida; always purchase nursery-grown plants.

planting.note: Scarify or soak seeds for 24 hours before direct sowing at the soil surface or just below in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, wet to moist soil near pond margins, streambanks, or rain garden depressions. Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow self-sows readily in favorable wet conditions.

directions: Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow is a tall, dramatic native perennial, native to the streambanks, marshy shores, and wet meadow edges of eastern North America from New York to Florida and west to Nebraska and Oklahoma, producing a succession of large, showy, white to pink flowers with a dark red center from July through September on stems reaching 6 feet in wet soil. The deeply three-lobed, halberd-shaped leaves distinguish it from the more common Swamp Rose Mallow (Hibiscus moscheutos), and the flowers are slightly smaller but equally attractive to hummingbirds and large native bees such as carpenter bees, which are among the few insects strong enough to force their way into the blossom. A perennial that dies to the ground in winter and re-emerges reliably from a stout root crown in spring, Halberd-leaf Rose Mallow is excellent for naturalizing the wet margins of ponds, detention basins, and rain gardens where its moisture requirement is easily met. Sow scarified seeds in place in wet soil in early spring, or divide established clumps.

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or plant nursery transplants in moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade. Glade Fern is often found naturally on moist limestone outcrops and in rich, calcareous woodland glades.

directions: Glade Fern is an unusual and distinctive native fern, native to the rich, moist, shaded glades, ravine floors, and calcareous woodland slopes of eastern North America from Quebec to Georgia and Tennessee, where it grows in the mineral-rich soils derived from limestone or dolomite that many ferns cannot tolerate. Unlike most ferns with finely divided pinnate fronds, Glade Fern produces long, strap-shaped, single-pinnate fronds with widely spaced, pointed pinnae that give it a loose, airy, elegant appearance quite different from the denser-textured wood ferns and lady ferns. A deciduous fern that dies back to the ground in fall and re-emerges in spring, it spreads slowly by short-creeping rhizomes to form loose colonies and is best used as a textural contrast plant among finer-textured shade ground covers. Grow in moist, humus-rich, neutral to slightly alkaline soil (pH 6.5–7.5) in partial to full shade; the preference for slightly alkaline conditions makes it a good companion to plants that enjoy limestone-derived soils.
sources: Native Ferns, Mosses and Grasses (William Cullina)

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Bahama Wild Coffee is a frost-tender native shade shrub suited to zones 9b–11 and an excellent understory plant under live oaks and tropical hardwoods in south Florida landscapes.

directions: Bahama Wild Coffee is a compact native evergreen shrub, native to the coastal hammocks and tropical hardwood forests of south Florida, the Keys, the Bahamas, and the Caribbean, where it grows as a common understory shrub beneath larger canopy trees in partial to full shade. Similar to Wild Coffee (Psychotria nervosa) but with smaller, shinier, privet-like leaves (hence the species epithet ligustrifolia, meaning privet-leaved), it produces small white flowers year-round that attract native bees and butterflies, followed by clusters of red berries that turn black and are avidly consumed by migratory warblers, thrushes, and robins in fall and winter. An outstanding native shade shrub for zone 9b–11 landscapes where its shade tolerance, modest size, year-round bloom, and exceptional wildlife value make it a versatile foundation plant, hedge component, or understory specimen. Grow in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; established plants tolerate brief drought but prefer consistent moisture.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline, rocky limestone or loamy soil in partial shade to filtered sun. Sycamore-leaf Snowbell is a Texas endemic adapted to the alkaline limestone soils of the Edwards Plateau and adjacent Hill Country.

directions: Sycamore-leaf Snowbell is a beautiful native understory shrub or small tree, endemic to the limestone canyons, creek banks, and rocky woodland slopes of the Texas Edwards Plateau and Hill Country, where it grows in the shaded understory of live oak and Texas cedar elm woodlands in thin, alkaline, limestone-derived soils. In April and May it produces clusters of fragrant, white, pendant bell-shaped flowers similar to those of other snowbells (Styrax species), with the blooms arranged more openly than the closely related American Snowbell (Styrax americanus). The large, maple-like (plataniform) leaves are a distinctive characteristic that sets it apart from other native snowbells. An excellent shrub for Texas Hill Country native landscapes and limestone-soil gardens in zones 7–9, it is drought-tolerant once established in well-drained alkaline soil and requires no fertilizer or supplemental irrigation after the first two seasons. Source from specialty native Texas plant nurseries.

planting.note: Scarify seeds lightly and direct sow in early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade. Virginia Mallow self-sows from year to year; established plants develop a persistent woody crown and re-sprout each spring.

directions: Virginia Mallow is a tall, bold native perennial, native to the rich, moist soils of bottomlands, riverbanks, woodland edges, and disturbed clearings from Virginia and Pennsylvania south to Georgia and west to Indiana, growing to 8–10 feet tall in fertile, moist soil and producing a continuous succession of small, five-petaled, white flowers from July through September that attract an exceptional diversity of native bees, including many specialist bees that are important pollinators. The large, palmately lobed leaves and towering height give Virginia Mallow a dramatic architectural quality in the native garden, providing bold structure in the back of borders and in naturalized meadow plantings. The abundant seeds are consumed by goldfinches, sparrows, and other seed-eating birds in fall and winter; the plant's stems provide nesting material and stem-nesting sites for solitary native bees. Grow in fertile, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; it is surprisingly adaptable and self-sows reliably once established.

planting.note: Plant nursery transplants or small divisions tucked into crevices of a rock garden or dry stone wall in partial to full shade, in neutral to slightly alkaline, freely draining, rocky or gritty soil. Maidenhair Spleenwort is a rock crevice specialist and thrives in the tight drainage and consistent humidity of dry stone walls and rocky outcrops.

directions: Maidenhair Spleenwort is one of the world's most widely distributed and delightful small ferns, native to rock crevices, cliff faces, and mortared stone walls throughout the temperate Northern Hemisphere from North America to Europe, Asia, and beyond, where its neat rosettes of tiny, dark-stemmed, pinnate fronds tuck into the smallest gaps between stones with remarkable grace. The evergreen, arching fronds — typically 4–8 inches long with rounded, alternating pinnae on wiry, dark, lustrous stems — are persistent through winter and provide delicate year-round texture in rock gardens, dry stone walls, and shaded rocky outcrops. Unlike most ferns that prefer moist, humus-rich soil, Maidenhair Spleenwort is adapted to excellent drainage and even slightly alkaline conditions derived from limestone or mortar — making it ideal for growing in rock garden crevices and the joints of dry stone walls, where its fine texture contrasts beautifully with the rugged stone. Plant in tight crevices with minimal amended soil and virtually no supplemental watering once established.
